reason: The statement applies because the text describes Nehemiah, a prophet, who becomes rich during a conflict with the enemies of the Hebrews. Nehemiah was appointed as governor of Judah and was involved in various economic activities, including overseeing the distribution of resources and managing the work on the wall of Jerusalem. He mentions his own wealth and how he did not take the usual privileges of the governor, showing that he accumulated wealth during his time in office. This aligns with the statement that there is a prophet who becomes rich during a conflict with the enemy of the Hebrews.
